agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order lifecycle management for a system where orders progress through placement, invoicing, and shipping. Three stakeholder groups have provided conflicting requirements about when cancellation is permitted.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, which progresses through a lifecycle of states from placement to shipment."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order so it will not be fulfilled or shipped. The precise business rules for when this is permitted are currently in dispute."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document that locks in pricing for an order. Finance considers invoicing irreversible."},{"term":"Placement","definition":"The initial act of creating an order in the system. Operations states invoicing occurs at this point."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer. PM considers this the cutoff point for cancellation."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a PM-defined business policy about cancellation windows. However, it directly contradicts BR-2 combined with BR-3.","testable_assertion":"UNTESTABLE — contradicts BR-2 + BR-3. If an order is invoiced at placement, and invoiced orders cannot be cancelled, then no order can ever be cancelled before shipping."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled. Invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance treats invoicing as a hard financial constraint — once revenue is recognized or pricing is locked via invoice, the financial record cannot be unwound through simple cancellation. This is likely tied to accounting integrity and possibly regulatory compliance.","testable_assertion":"UNTESTABLE in combination with BR-1 — the rules are mutually exclusive given BR-3."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy decision about when invoicing occurs. A VP could change the invoicing trigger point (e.g., invoice at shipment instead of placement). However, as currently stated, it creates a logical impossibility with BR-1.","testable_assertion":"Given a newly placed order, an invoice record must exist immediately. No order exists in a placed-but-not-invoiced state."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed and immediately cancelled","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — PM says this should succeed (not yet shipped), but Finance says it must fail (already invoiced at placement per Ops)."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment cancellation","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — if an order has multiple line items and only some have shipped, cancellation rules are unclear even without the core contradiction."},{"scenario":"Credit memo or refund as alternative to cancellation","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — Finance may distinguish between cancelling an invoice (forbidden) and issuing a credit memo against it (potentially allowed). This distinction is not addressed in the requirements."},{"scenario":"Price change between placement and shipment","expected_behavior":"Operations invoices at placement to lock pricing, but if cancellation-and-reorder is the workaround, the locked price is lost — defeating the purpose of early invoicing."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH — If we implement PM's rule and allow cancellation of invoiced orders, we break financial record integrity. If we implement Finance's rule strictly, customers can never cancel orders, leading to chargebacks, returns, and customer churn.","operational":"HIGH — Operations chose to invoice early for pricing stability. Any resolution must address whether that timing can change, or whether a compensating mechanism (credit memo, reversal) is acceptable.","user":"HIGH — Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment. A system that forbids all cancellation after placement will generate support tickets, negative reviews, and lost trust.","risk":"CRITICAL — Implementing any single interpretation without resolving the contradiction will either break financial integrity or destroy customer experience. Both carry regulatory and reputational risk.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders at all if Finance+Ops rules prevail. Will resort to chargebacks and returns, increasing cost."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"Revenue recognition and accounting integrity at risk if PM's rule is implemented without addressing invoicing."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"Early invoicing policy may need to change, affecting pricing lock-in workflows and downstream fulfillment processes."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the brunt of customer complaints regardless of which rule is implemented, unless the contradiction is resolved."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Must reconcile the cancellation promise with financial and operational realities before development can proceed."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct logical contradiction: BR-1 permits cancellation before shipment, but BR-2 + BR-3 together make cancellation impossible from the moment of placement. These three rules cannot all be true simultaneously. No acceptance criteria can be defined until stakeholders resolve this conflict.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and not a matter of interpretation — it is a logical impossibility","Invoicing and cancellation are both critical domain concepts that must have clear, consistent rules","All three stakeholder groups must be brought together to resolve this"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Finance would accept a credit memo or reversal as distinct from 'cancellation'","Whether Operations would accept moving invoicing to a later lifecycle stage","Whether there is an unstated distinction between 'cancellation' and 'invoice voiding' in this domain"],"assumptions":["'Invoiced' in BR-2 and 'invoice at placement' in BR-3 refer to the same concept of invoicing","There is no existing credit memo or invoice reversal process that the stakeholders consider distinct from cancellation","The word 'cancelled' means the same thing to all three stakeholder groups"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements form a logical impossibility. PM says orders can be cancelled before shipping. Finance says invoiced orders cannot be cancelled. Operations says orders are invoiced at placement. Therefore: orders are invoiced the instant they exist, can never be cancelled, yet PM requires they be cancellable. These three statements cannot all hold simultaneously.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["A: Move invoicing to shipment time (breaks Ops pricing lock-in requirement, but enables PM's cancellation window)","B: Introduce a 'pro-forma invoice' or 'quote' at placement that is not a true invoice, with the real invoice generated at shipment (preserves pricing lock-in, enables cancellation, may satisfy Finance)","C: Keep invoicing at placement but allow cancellation via credit memo / invoice reversal (Finance must agree that reversal is acceptable even though 'invoicing is irreversible')","D: Restrict cancellation to a short grace period before invoicing is finalized (requires Ops to delay invoicing by minutes/hours)"],"recommendation":"Option B — Separate pricing lock-in from formal invoicing. A pro-forma invoice or pricing commitment at placement locks in the price (satisfying Ops), while the legal/financial invoice is generated at shipment (satisfying Finance's irreversibility rule and PM's cancellation window). However, ALL THREE stakeholder groups must explicitly agree before development proceeds."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Logical contradiction across three stakeholder requirements makes the spec unimplementable as stated.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-2, and BR-3 are mutually exclusive. Any implementation that satisfies all three is impossible."}]}
